text: There's this idea of 'going with the flow' and a conflict between allowing things to be vs being too uncritically caught in that stream. I think that there can be a distinction between flowing *in* and flowing *with*. https://t.co/zqJVkwi3De
When we're flowing *in*, it's to be swept up and be embedded in. OTOH, when we're flowing *with*, it's that idea of letting it come, be, and go.

Flowing 'in' is to operate based on only one of the levels, which means we're disconnected from the others. However, this allows for a 'closeness' that is (perhaps) harder to get to in flowing 'with'. https://t.co/1bDX74QXPv
Flowing 'with' is to flow *across* the layers. It's an 'acceptance' in relation to a more integrated scope. https://t.co/2UAenJuFTU

This means that we're not blindly acting on whatever actions and thoughts and feelings that appear to us, nor are we only working with the underlying functions and patterns without considering how they manifest as actions, thoughts or feelings.
Ideally, we can simultaneously hold both the flowing 'with' mode, the 'gestalt', and the flowing 'in' mode, the 'feature'. It's sort of like the attention/ awareness difference.
I'm not entirely sure if this is the right frame though, because it feels to me that flowing 'with' could encompass flowing 'in'.

**Learning Thread**:
Environmental Psychology

EP talks about how we experience, conceptualise, interact with, and are embedded within space (environment).

Environmental Psych:
Week 1

We read a piece from Kurt Lewin (Psychological Ecology, 1943), who's referred to as the founder of social psychology. 

Some background: he was a Gestalt psychologist, a school that believed that we can't understand people from their parts and must look at their whole.

What did he consider to be 'the whole'?

He called it a person's **life space**, or **psychological field**. This is the combination of all the factors that, moment to moment, influence a person's *behaviour*. https://t.co/4AlThFH5Nf

The idea can be summed up in this equation:

person x environment = behaviour

Outside of our individual personalities etc, we are also, simultaneously, within any given moment, embedded within various environments that exert pressures on us.
Put another way (getting to the piece itself), it's a person's **psychological ecology**.

To understand behaviour, Lewin thought that we have to first study "[the] “nonpsychological” data to find out what these data mean for determining the boundary conditions of the life of the individual or group."

What are the external contexts that the individual is within?

Here, through the example of food, Lewin gives an exemplary description of what I call the 'going above' aspect of thinking more complexly. https://t.co/DOWOPjrrEc
He then furthers the analysis of "why people eat what they eat" through his **channel theory**.

The way I understand it is (with Wilber frame): channel theory first looks at the 'interobjective' section of forces, and then understands how those forces are controlled via the 'interior' dimensions.

I think about the theory applying a 'filter' on those forces so that a particular structure appears, which in this case is channels and gatekeepers. It helps us better identify and consider more factors.
In his words, channel theory looks at the problem of "where and how the psychological and the nonpsychological aspects intersect."
If we think about food, there are various channels by which we get food: https://t.co/05nrS1TtEV
The channels can change, be blocked, interact with one another, and so on. An example Lewin gives is: when making food at home becomes difficult, we may eat out at restaurants more.
Channels have **gatekeepers** (he was the one who coined this term!)

For instance, my mum was the one who got and prepared all the food, and so the 'food channel' at home for me (as a singular chunk of 'meals') was controlled by her, i.e where the food was sourced from, how it was prepared, etc.

The 'psychological' (or 'interior') part comes in when we consider what determines how the gatekeeper gatekeeps.

He puts it under two broad categories: **cognitive structure** and **motivation**.
Continuing with the food theme, **cognitive structure** would be how we think and talk about food.

What is considered 'food' vs 'food for us'? Wrapped in this are cultural factors — what is *culturally available* to us. 

For instance, the first thing that came to my mind are 'chicken feet'. Lewin gives the example of live grasshoppers.
Another aspect might be meal patterns — oatmeal and waffles might feel strange to eat at dinner.
A third might be belongingness, where the social context of where the food is eaten changes the meaning of eating — how we eat, and what we eat for. Here's Lewin: https://t.co/cSDlN4AAOZ

On the **motivation** side, it includes things like our system of values (motives, ideologies — he gives an interesting example of food under wartime, or difference between restaurant vs at home), and ...
... what obstacles needed to be overcome — in a study they did, they found that canned food was preferred because it can be prepared quicker.
In sum, here's one way to visualise these forces: https://t.co/qvcPOauDjE
https://t.co/yukAbMlVdr

[Learning Thread]

Tai Chi, Qigong, and Traditions of Energy Cultivation in China

https://t.co/ur7ebZT7NL

Traditions of Energy Cultivation in China:
Week 1

In the first week, I learned about: general Daoist concepts, apophatic (taking away) meditation, and this exchange between the yellow emperor and a guru. 

https://t.co/tcnkU5kHVo
https://t.co/JcJ5nrbssc
https://t.co/tcnkU5kHVo
There's _so_ many interesting things and I wish I could just do an info dump, but I don't think that will be very readable, and much of it is intro material that I'm sure is readily found elsewhere.

That said, here are some things that I found particularly noteworthy, mostly drawing from a chapter written by Harold Roth on Daoist Apophatic Meditation + the lecture. The QT always applies, but particularly so here.

https://t.co/VNUfvXqGsy

There's this idea that self-cultivation gets us to our most natural state such that we embody the Dao and become in complete accordance with the patterns/ natural order (li, 理) of the world. https://t.co/wGHw2Qwmgs

I'm curious as to what the relation of the 'self' is here — does the Dao become the self (like resting in Awareness)? Or is it an integration of a larger perspective like how we 'become the sage'?

Of particular relevance is this comparison of 'Oneness': https://t.co/ByQkTtuRkv

I ask this because there's a continual emphasis on being without selfishness, of stepping outside of our own egos and limited and fixed points of view, and so what is the interaction here?

Two diagrams on union and unity. https://t.co/eEEvI01qAO

"Rather, one goes through a discipline and has experiences that allow one to view the world in a new way" — this sounds very 'Vervaekian', where there's a shift in *how* we make sense of the world (relationship), rather than *what* we propositionally know (content). https://t.co/G78SqXzZ0L

Focusing a bit more on the 'naturalness' part, the Zhuangzi has this idea of 'flowing cognition', which is where we are 'totally changing and transforming to the situation' in a way that's spontaneous and (again) natural (zi ran, 自然).
This is what 'wu wei' points at, where nothing is done from the self, and yet nothing is left undone.
This 'zi ran'-ness is 'frequently associated with a great deal of inner power', i.e 'de' (the 'de, 德' in Dao De Jing), which is a material expression of the Dao. https://t.co/u6M3YH2Osb

In our most natural state, our actions will be always in harmony with whatever situation we found ourselves in. This is very different from the e.g Hobbesian view of 'human nature', and I wonder if I'm making a category error in comparing the two.

Nevertheless, might we say that the Hobbesian version **is** 'nature', but it's 'natural' at the uncultivated level? This reminds me of @sbkfaufman in Transcend, where it prompted in me this idea that what human nature 'is' changes depending on which needs we have fulfilled.

Moving on, an idea that struck me like lightning was the Confusion one of 'ren, 仁'. This is a moral value where you want the best for others and for everyone involved in the interaction to reach their highest potential for flourishing.
Like many characters in Chinese, the very structure of the word poetically conveys its meaning (like the word meta-phor). The left hand side is 'person', the right hand side 'two'.

This notion is precisely one of my guiding stars (intertwined with agape)! Heavily inspired by Vervaeke, I strive to act in a way such that I can best afford the transformation and flourishing of others.
I'm reminded of Daniel Schmachtenberger, who often talks about the alignment of every agent's incentives with every other agent's wellbeing.

DS talks even more explicitly about the exact idea behind 'ren' in 'Health Index' at the Stoa (@peternlimberg), where he muses on how the English language doesn't have an equivalent word for this concept. On @lexfridman, he refers to it as being 'stoked' for others.

I believe @JeremyRLent talks about neo-confucianism in his book 'The Patterning Instinct'. I wonder if he refers to 'ren'? Once I find the time to read the book, I'll be sure to update this thread if it does show up.
A misc observation is that one 'purpose' of cultivation in Daoism seems to be in harmony with the World (which is an end in itself), whereas in Buddhism it's to be free from suffering.
Very different starting points! Yet the particular style of Daoist meditations we read about this week maps very closely onto some Buddhist practices.

I've wanted to do a movement practice for a while now to complement my sitting practice. I'm currently taking a uni class in tai chi, and so it was the perfect excuse.

If you're curious about the uni class, here's some thoughts from the first week:

https://t.co/JcJ5nrt3QM
This thread will be much looser than the other ones, and I'll add to it over time as interesting things come up.

---

I did the first class yesterday evening. I had only done ~two hours total of tai chi from that uni class, but there were already some interesting things I want to share
(I'll probably be mixing in ideas from the uni class throughout)

One thing that my professor emphasised is the importance of the legs/ feet. Sure, the fancy stuff happens up top with the hands, but the way you know who's the real deal is by seeing how good their balance is.
And that balance is kept in _every moment of the form_, throughout every movement and every transition sequence!

He got us to feel through that via walking the tai chi way where rather than how we normally walk of continually falling forwards into the next step, the shifting of weight is much more deliberate

https://t.co/xTgbjuOQ8m

Off the top of my head, the other neat thing was that of effortlessness in e.g the tai chi way of standing . Despite having your legs slightly bent and your arms out in front of you, for the experienced, there's minimal muscle used — people stand for _hours_ like this! https://t.co/2wg248NnGP
(Tasshin actually has a great post on utilizing that posture for meditation: https://t.co/iZk7I5qZCo)
Onto the sun tai chi class itself, the biggest thing for me was a strong sense of 'embodiment', which felt _really_ good
I think it's the first time I (my body) felt so consciously 'material' and that I'm moving through this very tangible stuff of 'space'.

I could _feel_ the air, and it felt a bit like moving my hands through water

That there's this real sensory 'feedback' makes me feel more connected to the physical world, and what's exciting for me is tasting the 'flavour' of that connection

Moving my entire body so deliberately brought up a particular feeling of it being 'unified' and working together

And all it took was 10 seconds total of movements!
It's also fun to just shift my weight around and consciously feel my body 'flow' into the ground in different ways

Also, I don't know why, but there's this small movement where we 'open up' the feet or keep it 'empty' — the back heel is lifted off slightly from the floor — and something about it seems super cool to me!
https://t.co/5ccKdGhQqQ

Wonderful — looking forward to delving into both!

Environmental Psych:
Week 2

https://t.co/6hKVgFObym
I want to talk about personal space + non-persons, and riff on the idea of affordances + the space of possibility (a central concept in my thinking).
This week also covered our perceptions of cities. I'll be discussing that in another thread (linked at the end) related to space in the digital world.

First: personal space. This is from Robert Sommer's _Spatial Invasion_. 

We might think of personal space as 'portable territory' that expands and contracts dependent on context, for example in our own private bedroom vs on the streets of NYC.

I'm particularly fascinated by Sommer's description of us having various _states_ of personal space, its relation to self, and this notion that "[p]ersonal space is a culturally acquired daylight phenomenon."

If we have a comfortable amount of personal space, we feel more so our full selves as we now have "privacy, dignity and individuality". In this state, the very awareness of 'having' that personal space fades into the background.
We can see this in intimate situations: we feel willing and safe for others to enter into our personal space. https://t.co/ZZGQ1xpoFJ

However, allowing people to come close to us doesn't always mean we feel safe and intimate with them. In fact, those tight situations in e.g the elevator are conversely when we're particularly aware of our personal space.
Consider this delightful example of the subway: https://t.co/KHHzTugiwy
During class, one reason given for why we act in that way is because of the unpredictability of the kinds of people whom we're riding public transport with. The possibility of danger is salient.
Additionally, there seems to be a rejection of that subway-self as 'us'. If many of us spend so much time in public spaces 'frozen' in these states, how is that shaping our psyche?

I wonder if, given far safer conditions and more tight-knit culture, spontaneous lively conversations with strangers might become a possible norm? 

As an introvert, the thought of that terrifies.

As I look at the heartbreaking statistics on loneliness however, something in this ballpark (e.g in other public spaces such as the park?) might be worth investigating. At the very least, thinking about how we 'do' 'warmer' public spaces would yield useful insights.

Adding more to the subway example, Sommer notes how as part of our psychological withdrawal, we treat "prefer to treat other riders as nonpersons." This is because "[a] nonperson cannot invade someone's personal space any more than a tree or chair can." https://t.co/RKVBtzRMkl
However, in situations of emergency, people become real once again: https://t.co/9NRa647GRB

I had to do a group presentation on our articles this week, and for part of it I linked these local-scale 'emergencies' to our global-scale pandemic, specifically the emergence of various communal things like neighbours delivering food or a street wide sing-along.

In situations like these, our neighbours, whom we might've treated as just a familiar face we pass by, an 'object' in many respects, turn into 'persons' whom we have reciprocally nourishing relationship with.

---

Second: affordances. We read James J. Gibson's _The Theory of Affordances_. 

I was already familiar with this concept via Vervaeke, but it was still nice to read Gibson's actual paper.

For those unfamiliar, here's the definition:

>  The *affordances* of the environment are what it *offers* the animal, what it *provides* or *furnishes*, either for good or ill.
The way I look at it is that things provide / allow for certain possibilities, whether those possibilities are to see or to fall or to be warm or to be more loving etc.

When we talk about the affordances that something has, I define that as the _space of possibilities_ that something provides. When a chair affords sitting, this means that within the chair's space of possibilities, sitting on it is an option.

I think affordances are created through the interaction between an agent and something else (chair, person, animal, environment etc). 

Self + Chair -> Possibilities https://t.co/Vw1oLFdKCu

Gibson points to how it seems that "[t]he meaning is observed before the substance and surface", i.e we care first about what we can do with a thing before we care about its physical qualities like shape and colour. https://t.co/zdG2BqSPKL
Another thing that stuck out is that 'behaviour affords behaviour' — it's not just 'things' that afford behaviour, but also _acts_ and _processes_.

All the above feels obvious, but has a lot of implications when it comes to something like: how is our lifestyle affording our wellbeing? Is Netflix the only option we have to relax, or do we have a broader space of possibility that we've cultivated?
(These ideas are heavily inspired by @edelwax and his brilliant piece https://t.co/ROhJSdtjtp)
People don't veg out on TV after a long day of work because they're lazy, but (in part) because they don't have any other good and equally easy possibility available to them.
Cultivating these possibilities is what I believe Joe (at least in part) is doing with his 'tools for feeling' and his building of a 'second heart': https://t.co/qx8GeQCTRD + https://t.co/9vPM1U9Ecd
(Which, as a sidenote, completely blew my mind when I first saw it because it's _exactly_ in an intersection between digital tools and self-self related stuff that I'm trying to explore)

In what ways does e.g our *culture* afford us in working with what's True, Good, Beautiful? 

How has the way we've been raised afforded us various possibilities (or lack of possibilities) in reaction to our kid drawing on the wall?
One of the best things this idea has done is to see that there is this space, and that it can actually be expanded! The options we have become one among many known but also unknown alternatives!

Another aspect of this is that the notion itself has afforded me to be open in seeing how there's real possibilities in every problem and solution that goes beyond our common conceptions. Things like 'both-and' helps push me further in this direction.
That's all for now. This idea of 'space of possibility' has been a gift that keeps on giving and I refer to it all over the place, and so I hope it's at least somewhat useful to you too.
https://t.co/TpXvbkMowx
@nickcammarata Warm sun on back as we got ready to do tai chi :))

---

Once in a while, after some time into the session, there's a feeling of 'rightness' to certain moves. Some feel fluid and good, for example the raising of arms, while others, like the dropping of them, feel airy and awkward.
There's a sense of some movements being solid and stable like mahogany, while others that are too light, like cheap plastic toys
I don't know if this intuitive sense of 'rightness' even maps onto the proper movements — there's so much subtlety to every move that I'm far off from learning yet
I get the sense that with practice, all the moves will eventually feel 'solid' and like I'm moving along the grooves of a wood, rather than against its grain.

---

I got to learn a characteristic movement of sun tai chi last week, which is where the hands are in a prayer-like position but close to the body and with the space of a small ball in between, and they expand out horizontally and then back in with the breath

It feels so good to move with the breath! Somewhere in his chat with Tasshin, Stanwood talks about how here in the West, we usually talk about the mind and the body, but almost never about the breath

https://t.co/0i9Ei9aUcv
I had played around a little with Wim Hof style breathing (the recommended timings and repetitions didn't really do anything to me), and have focused on breath sensations via TMI, but ...

... having the mind + body + breath harmonising together has _such_ a different quality to it -- it makes the entire thing much more alive! The breath no longer becomes an object, but is part of an integrated flow / process
Very excited to get to the second pass of the form, which is when I can start learning the breath patterns for the rest of the movements!

For the final presentation of my Environmental Psychology class, I thought about how some of the ideas we learnt over the semester could be related to the web.

I'm naming the cluster of ideas _Making a Place on the Web_. https://t.co/VlhEBRegfM
I've selected and expanded on ~3 main parts from that presentation, each in its own thread.

(This is the learning thread for that environmental psych class, where I summarise some concepts I liked from each week's readings.)

https://t.co/6hKVgFObym
https://t.co/F27qYsO5Cl
https://t.co/yodbrJT6dF

I want to meander over a few topics: the idea of 'home' and making a cozier digital in the form of bedrooms and living rooms, home as self, the private and the public, three kinds of co-created spaces on the web, and my desire for a co-created space as 'garden'. https://t.co/k4Suw5KHcJ
I don't really have any answers or proposals, but I think the ideas here help richen the flowerbed.

This thread is one of three on the idea of 'making a place on the web'. 

https://t.co/9lhPsIbqDz
It's also in essay format here: https://t.co/t1g9fUDxmM
To begin, we often talk about creating a home on the internet. What does 'home' mean?
Drawing from _The People, Place, and Space Reader_, we typically associate homes with buildings, and Heidegger argues that it's the way in which we 'dwell' within buildings that make it a home.

The word 'building' has its roots in the Old English / High German version 'baun', which means 'to dwell'. The German words for 'am' and 'are', as in 'I am' (ich bin) and 'you are' (du bist) also relates to 'buan', so 'I dwell' and 'you dwell'.
To be at home isn't merely to occupy a space, but relates to how we are *rooted* within it, and not just as any creature, but specifically as humans.

Here's Heidegger: 

> The way in which you are and I am, the manner in which we humans are on the earth, is buan, dwelling. To be a human being means to be on the earth as a mortal. It means to dwell.
It isn't just a 'house'. As Rybczynski says in _Domesticity_, "you could walk out of the house, but you always returned home." https://t.co/G4Of1eYeCT

That Rybczynski piece is fantastic btw, it covers how our modern notion of 'domesticity', of the private family home, the 'housewife' (who's strict about keeping the home clean), of taking off shoes when entering home etc had strong historical roots in 17th century Netherlands.
One notion that stuck with me is this beautiful idea of how a song could be home. From J. Macgregor Wise's _Home_: https://t.co/yNSRlOP8gA

So the home is existentially grounding, a place of comfort that "permits people to fashion in their own image", a "setting for personal seclusion and intimate behaviour free from public comment or restraint." 

(Imrie in _Disability, Embodiment and the Meaning of the Home_)

It's striking, then, how little control most of us have over the vast majority of our digital space. 

Sure, we could move around a few files and maybe post a few things, but that's about the extent to which we can change this environment.

I want a space where I can really shape it so that, for example, the first thing I see when I opened up my laptop is a livestream of a place that's currently having its sunrise. https://t.co/RRpv7odAVJ
What opens up when we think about the desktop as a bedroom?

I like the idea of friends visiting my room, and maybe there's a cookie jar where they can randomly take a tweet or a photo from a curated collection. What if they could also leave a note for when I return? https://t.co/oJAJLT8NYR
Imrie has this concept of each part of the home as serving some physical and mental function: https://t.co/0rG7NG3wtB

The digital is (currently) capable of serving the mental. How do we do that better? Along which dimensions? 

How might such 'zones' be reflected in the interface? How can we (users) actively shape such zones?
It's curious to consider how the digital might provide genuine rest and recuperation.
With how strongly bound the 'home' is to our existential senses, it makes perfect sense that it becomes, as Clare Cooper describes, relating to Jung, a symbol for the self.

Beyond the body, she argues, the house is what we frequently select to "represent or symbolize what is tantalizingly unrepresentable."

(_The House as a Symbol of the Self_) https://t.co/dKNYTbGNzJ
The house has a dual aspect: its interior and façade, at once enclosing and excluding. https://t.co/2luNYzmwkZ
More broadly, she describes two notions of the house: house as defensive fortress vs house as expression of self-and-family. https://t.co/5tdpkS5e64
So ideally, the home would allow for such dual aspects of retreat (comfort) as well as expression (to be able to represent the unrepresentable).

Returning to the 'bedroom' notion, it would make sense to have a 'private-private' (interior), and an option to have an 'invite-only private' (still interior) that can be more 'cleaned up'. https://t.co/CXjUDgTR6o

That invite-only private space would be one presentation of the self. 

What if all those various social medias, each a different mask / façade we create, are connected to our 'bedroom' in some explicit way? https://t.co/OfdfSQYa3B
By making explicit this singular 'private' self that has different 'public' instantiations, one effect might be greater salience of the different 'masks' that we wear and hold.

This is interesting to me for various reasons, one of which is that it might bring greater awareness to these ideas of being authentic to *ourselves*, bringing questions of why we are presenting the way we are presenting, and so on.
Expanding the home, what if our room is connected to a 'living room'? I'm thinking of a more modifiable sort of group chat, with 'channels' as rooms. https://t.co/sOVKayM8ig

This thread is about the idea of gardening, and how sending digital care packages + going on and sharing internet strolls and rabbit holes might be two small ways to garden on the web. https://t.co/1BakILcASE

I want to start with thinking about 'gardening' through Michael Pollan's essay _Beyond Wilderness and Lawn_. In it, he distinguishes three ways of treating nature: as lawn, as wilderness, and as garden.

'Lawn' is nature as put under complete control, thoroughly domesticated, tightly cropped. 

'Wilderness' is nature as sacred, something to be worshiped, to be left untouched.

It's a fascinating essay, covering the history of how the idea of the 'lawn' originated in ~1870 America, which was the exact same time the transcendentalists like Emerson and Thoreau were pushing forward 'wilderness' (national parks came about then).

Pollan then argues that both approaches fall short of being able to solve our environmental problems. While gardens themselves won't put us in right relationship to nature, the "habits of thought they foster can take us a long way in that direction."
It forces us to "ask the gardener's questions", "[tutoring] us in nature's ways". https://t.co/qnu3K4QXZO

I want to think about Pollan's three *ways of relating*, and swap out the focus of 'nature' to 'digital objects'.

Gardening then becomes a way of working with the digital objects we collect, manage, create etc. https://t.co/jOoLPc0JXt

(by digital objects I'm meaning something roughly like below. Imported objects are things that can exist outside of the digital environment. Native things are created from, exists on, and only makes sense in the context of the digital environment) https://t.co/9DWvId3iPC

Let me give some examples. 

'Wilderness' might be a Twitter search with just the letter 'a'. This is pristine, untouched, chaotic information stream. There's definitely better examples to reflect data as unfiltered (since Twitter moderates), and raw data as sacred. https://t.co/aNzM49G4oN
'Lawn' might be some corporate website, where every pixel and word is considered and has gone through 10 different teams and months of meetings and reviews. https://t.co/JJMOdIvlct

It could be argued that something like an https://t.co/V1OXGKmibF profile that has all their channels neatly organised is a 'garden', although I think it still mostly leans towards wilderness because it's often just collection without tending. https://t.co/sDafrmuqnD
For me, gardening requires meaningful *revisiting* and *cultivating* over time — there's *growth* involved.

(I should note that none of these modes are 'better' or 'worse' than the other! It just depends on what you're trying to do with the information. I happen to be making a case for the garden mode here.)

The most direct example of a digital garden would be, well, digital gardens! Lead by folks like @andy_matuschak, they're personal, wiki-like spaces that collect ever growing, in-process notes. They're like a far less 'lawned up' blog — densely tangled, interlinked and *alive*. https://t.co/ZBeoJpsyb9
They deserve a separate thread, I love them. The mindset of e.g notes as evergreens has actually prompted me to ask better questions and take better notes.

For now I'll plug @jhooks essay: https://t.co/HV3IvqPMnF and @Mappletons repo which has a wealth of articles, theories, tools, *and* a directory of gardens! https://t.co/NOJmprw7Im

(both are great gardeners too!)

Before I move on, here's a graph. From the great Wild, we collect digital objects. Two examples of modes of collecting might be hoarding or curation. Finally, we then further treat them in a 'gardening' or a 'lawn' way. https://t.co/nyzgcDdIdY

With that, I was curious as to what other ways the garden concept might manifest digitally. I'll look at it through the conceptualisation above: revisiting + growing = gardening. https://t.co/YR82iVf1ZC

(I should say that I'm not particularly convinced by the two buckets since they blend into one another and it feels like growing / evolution is more constitutive to gardening than revisiting is, but for the sake of discussion let's say that they're helpful)

In 'revisiting', we might have spaced repetition or coming across the same ideas repeatedly through research over an extended period of time. Here's a compelling essay by @michael_nielsen on that: https://t.co/j8miqCMS5z
'Growing' might be accumulation on top of a foundation through developing ideas by e.g writing an essay about it, or through modes of threading (e.g @visakanv style) / through the backlinks of Roam.

When we talk about something with others, I think there's potential for both:

revisiting: sharing, explaining
growing: discussion

The first surrounds ideas on home, the home-as-self, and co-created spaces. 

https://t.co/DDK2Hm3BxQ

The thread is a mix of theory from the readings, and loose sketches on ideas like having a central 'bedroom' for our digital activities and having 'living rooms' that we could share with friends and family.

The second thinks through the idea of gardens as a way of organising the digital objects we collect on the web (and comparing them to the 'lawn' and 'wilderness' modes). 

It then considers 2 small ways to encourage the 'gardening' mode.

https://t.co/4A8Pnsqqd5
The question is: how do we incentivise gardening, including for non text-based things? Why would anyone *want* to revisit the 851st block in their arena channel? https://t.co/whJD4YnFFC

I think an answer might be through one of the ways we show care to our friends through the internet: 

sending memes! 

or resources, or songs, or papers https://t.co/3ywem31zzY

And so here's the idea — what if we made a 'care package' at the start of every month of the best stories, poems, podcasts that we've come across, maybe add a little note to each of them, and then send it off to our friend? And what if they did the same for us? https://t.co/ecyQaqZj1p

And on top of that, to link in the ideas of talking about it with others, what if our messaging apps had this integrated so we could start and maintain conversations based on the material that we've sent, and be able to link all these different threads together in some way too?
It allows us to revisit the material we've gotten over the past month, and perhaps even build on the same digital objects over multiple months.
I want to at some point build on top of @maxkriegers ideas on chat, covered in his wonderful comic: https://t.co/5i978bldsg
Moving on, one central part of the care packages is experiencing something together. I think a related idea here would be that of 'internet strolls and rabbit holes'. https://t.co/OB0TGfV4pK

Maybe on the 'door' that connects your bedroom to the living room, some of the material that you've collected over the day can show up, and so when they ask you how your day was, you could walk them through all the cool scenery that you've come across.

https://t.co/UEo7857KzL
I want to stay with this idea of co-created spaces for a while longer. At the moment, I think we've got maybe three types of co-created spaces on the internet: https://t.co/aPuCwg9FLa

The first are internet forums and communities. There's at least two types.

One is of the Reddit sort where you've got moderators that enforce these rules.
Here, I'm thinking about John Brinkerhoff Jackson's public square, with how they do the rules and the wikis as the symbols of what kind of community it is.

The idea is that the public square (with its churches, monuments, the-tree-planted-by-the-first-settlers) exists to at once reinforce the individual identity and also to "remind people of their civic privileges and duties — and tacitly to exclude the outsider."
The other is of the private slacks and discords, the "dark forests" so to speak: https://t.co/bZLqOenMA1
The second are spaces created through aggregation, for instance on Twitter — the space that's 'created' is based on who you and who the people you follow, follow.
There, you have 'corners' of Twitter that all follow one another and have these open back and forths. There's an evident 'culture' that emerges.
The third kind is that of teams / groups / collectives that work together to make a front — usually a website.
When I'm thinking about that living room, and inviting guests over to it — I want it to be something where there's a stronger sense of co-creation. https://t.co/Qrfveqkb9G
Where just by looking at the space, you get a sense of the different mixes, the different 'fingerprints' of people.

Something that's more of a garden, and less like a lawn. 

Something that meaningfully evolves over time, less coherent perhaps, but richer and more handmade.

https://t.co/4A8Pnsqqd5

Merleau-Ponty has this lovely line where he says that "the body is not in space like things; it inhabits or haunts space". 

How might we make a space that's worth haunting?
More importantly, if we can't "fashion" the space "in our own image", can we ever really create a home that we can truly dwell in?
I think that in working on shaping better homes, we can get better at creating other spaces that wrinkles the eyes and make them twinkle.
One day, I'd love to visit everyone's own unique campfires that affords them the comfort to relish in and freedom to express out delights of the universe. https://t.co/bkeAQN5sWg

Postscript:

I love Molly's provocation here. What happens when we shift our care from home-type spaces onto other ones? How would we like to 'dwell' within those?

https://t.co/Az5s6H4X5Z

when we shift from old to new (behaviours, technologies etc), there's always something lost or sacrificed. 

what was lost? 
is it worth figuring out a new way to fulfill that too? (and sometimes it's not!)

nothing only does one thing

the ideas i'm drawing inspiration from:

IFS idea that even the 'bad' things we do are for good reasons

mcluhan's idea that new technologies amputate as much as they amplify

chesterton's fence

to draw it out:

let's say old thing fulfilled 7 things for you, and the new thing does 5 them in a much better way

the 2 things left behind sometimes come from maladaptive reasons, but they served important functions for us

the goodness of the new thing may overshadow / cover up the still unfulfilled needs that those 2 things served

and eventually we might be a little confused as to why we feel like the new thing is clearly awesome and yet is still not quite there

i signed up for singing lessons cause i wanted to stop sounding like a duck, but with just the first lesson it's already given me more than i thought
(obligatory disclaimer that i have no idea what i'm talking about and might be misremembering what my teacher said)
first thing is the idea that giving space to the breath and singing through the notes is primary, rather than manipulating tone

another is that it's by _relaxing control_ do we get space, which actually increases range and 'actual' control

https://t.co/iMnXZ5W5LY

what i mean by this is that we usually think we sing through contorting our throat muscles in various ways

turns out that's not the main thing and actually tightens us up

rather, we want to ground ourselves in diaphragmatic breath, which is naturally, always there! 

(something something Awareness)
another is that singing should feel good! if it doesn't, we're doing something wrong

the idea that we all have this natural instrument, and that everyone's instrument is different - this is also super neat because even though it's all "voice" - we all have it constructed slightly differently, and working with it feels different to everyone

some reflections on the actual experience of singing:
it's really fun! 

embodied vibrations are nice to experience, and at some point i felt like laughing which was really interesting - maybe singing is helping me loosen up in various parts?

there's also this thing of just hollering out the note and being OK with my voice cracking in the passaggio (which btw what a poetic term for pointing to that concept) 

trusting in the process, leaning into discomfort, relaxing into screwing up

i think the idea that (self) acceptance results in complacency comes in part from believing that we can only deeply accept things that are perfect
i mean perfect in the sense that something is 1. flawless and so 2. no longer needs to change, which 3. follows that to change means it becomes something else and so is no longer perfect

often, the belief that something doesn't need to change is not separated from flawlessness

hoping someone becomes better gets tied to us being dissatisfied with them in some way - they are 'lacking' (not perfect), often in some core way

there's a few pieces here

one is that we can see the flaws in someone and reject them more as they're now less worthy for that, or we can see that same lack and still accept them just as deeply as before

another is that wishing the flourishing of someone can come not from dissatisfaction or expectation but from a "I hope to see you flourish because it'll bring *you* greater joy", a "I'm *rooting* for your blossoming"

so accepting you the way you are and not thinking you *ought* to change in any way is totally compatible with "may you grow into your moreness", and both are removed from the judgement of flaws as tied to their worth as people
I think acceptance is maybe better put not so much as "you are perfect the way you are", but rather "I take you as you are, in your entirety"
I see the entirety of your being — your golden light, your twisted darkness — I take that all in, and I accept that as it is.

this requires a clarity in perception and great tenderness

I would argue that acceptance is foundational for deep love
we can of course only accept parts of people - and that's OK! Then it's scoped to accepting that part in its entirety

friendships that nourish us are rooted in a fullness of acceptance

week 2 singing things!

we were playing with the bridge where the 'normal' singing voice turns into the falsetto and figuring out some ways to transition that smoothly

a key thing my teacher said:
the voice wants to crack - trust it, lean into it
voice cracks happens because we're blocking what the voice naturally wants to do, and when we're on that precipice we pull crack and then rapidly pull back

another is that the falsetto will most likely sound weird in our own heads, so:

trust how it feels! rather than how it sounds

some interesting technical things of singing "into" various parts - somehow that aligns our mind + body well, such as how she suggested to "keep the voice up rather than dropping it down as the tone drops down"

or to sing into the roof of the mouth, into that area of the nose / upper lip

neat to see this kinda concept that i'm familiar with from whole body breathing meditation stuff show up here

one last idea (in my way of putting it): 

it's the same force that animates the singing - it all comes from the diaphragm, comes from the same breath

we're just doing different things with e.g the mouth formations (for vowels etc) to sculpt that sound

karaoke preserves ritual of communal singing, which is not about singing but abt the bonding *through* singing

being raised in the west, i'm very self-conscious of singing bc it feels vulnerable to do as it's focused on the self - off notes reflect worth-laden lack of ability
otoh there is something real about voice as intimate expression, but there hadn't been a place for me where that expression is divorced from the question of if it actually sounds good

so i'm frustrated when i'm egged on to 'just sing! it's no big deal!' because it actually _is_ a big deal, because there hasn't been a situation where it felt safe to do that kind of thing, especially when i _know_ i sound shit

that safety could've come from at least 2 sources

1 - i'm just that sorta person who has a confidence in self expression and doesn't mind looking stupid and being goofy etc (hahahahaha no)

2 - being in contexts where i look (sound) stupid and it being totally OK and maybe even expected but that's fine bc that's not the point!

many such cases - dance is another one for me

as plenty others have observed, it's criminal that something so fundamental to being a human - the expression of voice and body - has been reduced to anything less than a joyful act for everyone

so conclusion is that we ought to have more places where we can be profoundly stupid and it being fine because the point isn't whether or not we look stupid but rather that we're _doing_ it with people we adore

look, why do we create? why do _you_ create? why not do literally anything else?

seems totally irrational to spend time creating pretty things that make people cry if instead you could advance being a Useful member of society

but have you ever heard a softly blooping stream? feel a baby's smile? seen cascading wind swept leaves that shimmer green and gold, anguished tears trace a cheekbone, a rose that blooms just so?

let's put that aside for a moment

what do we mean when we say that a piece of art is bad? 

3 views: 
1. the art is technically inproficient (it doesn't read smoothly, values are scattered)
2. the art does not resonant (don't like it, doesn't make sense)
3. the art is not from the heart (feels fake / inauthentic)

there's a moral angle of "but what if terrible person made evil art that harmed many" - i'm putting that aside because YOU dear maker (reader) is not evil, and this thread is about YOU not them

anyways
for view 1, the 20th century with your pollocks and dada poets has shown through and through that technical proficiency doesn't determine art being good, and it's not _just_ about novelty either
but here is where you get people pointin' at a picasso and going "my five year old could draw that! how is that good art!!"

my brother in christ a ROCK can be a profoundly poetic piece of art - how is it that something created from the smol hands of an entire, birthed, blood and flesh human being who's giggled and cried on this planet for five (5) 五!!! cycles around the sun not "real" and "good" art?

from the Impro angle, making art to the inventive spontaneous caliber of 5 year olds is actually really hard! 

this excerpt (that talks abt 8 y.o) encapsulates it perfectly - it's an excellent excerpt from an excellent book https://t.co/tCYJncx39S

from the art school angle, try making abstract art yourself! no, not even *sell* any, make some! make 100 for yourself, make 100 trying to represent what those artists you're poo-pooing on is trying to represent through their lens (figure, music-on-paper, basic block of reality)
i think you'll realise that making interesting looking abstract marks and lines and shapes that don't quickly get visually dull and derivative after the 10th one is not an easy task
the stuff those abstract artists pulled out of their ass actually has some merit - it's not purely just a money laundering scheme

(there's the flipside of all this where hyperrealistic art is deemed as soulless, and while they're not my jam i think there's a case for them too, but that's another train of thought

also i'm using lots of visual art eg, but i hope u see it applies more broadly too)

so a very legitimate point that can be raised is that e.g a piece of writing that is hard to read is bad art

now *here* we're getting into something more interesting

the question is: what does technicality serve?

i've spilled a good amount of ink writing a college paper on what i think is happening when we call something 'poetic'.
a point i argued is that things like rhyme and meter and metaphors are all tools that make having an experience of (e.g) a poem as poetic more 'accident prone'
i'm taking 'accident prone' here from meditation circles where people say that we meditate not to definitively reach awakening, but rather to make it more likely to accidentally happen
likewise, technical stuff helps us better make the impact we're hoping to make - but what *are* we hoping to do?
before that, to stay with the 'make impact' part some more, let's loop in the second view where 'bad' art is something that doesn't resonant (don't like it, don't get it)

we only need to think of the van gogh's and kafka's - heck, there's entire groups of people who hated EEAAO! 

personal resonance, even a BIG group of people's resonance, doesn't christen some art to be good and others bad
what is Good lies beyond the mutterings of the masses!

week 3 had a substitute teacher, we noodled around some notes which was fun, but nothing noteworthy
don't punch the note, take it on a date!
week 4's singing lesson surrounded many ideas of strain and ease - the teacher's comments around where we often get stuck is reminding me of how visceral of an activity singing is

also, the not-so-good tensing and 'trying' approach by which I do things in other areas transfer over into something as seemingly low stake as singing 

(low stake when i'm doing it alone / with the teacher)

interestingly, as i'm continually discovering, the best ways to sing also works really well as general principles to be guided by

nested fractals - what's Good is found in fullness among parts and wholes

so, i'm getting more familiar with the passaggio, which seems to be the Big Scary Thing that many beginners have to deal with

in fact, the common responses to it are the classic 3:
fight, flight, freeze

i'm told i take the "fight" approach lmao

what that means is i vocally white knuckle my way through it 

instead, i was reminded again that singing should actually be spacious, tranquil, fluid, which my teacher admits is difficult when i'm fighting for my life trying to get my voice to break through

some fun imagery to work with those higher notes:

bite into it like an apple
lean into it like a fish hook
open up like you're just about to sneeze

another tip she gave me was to go with the note, to be with it the whole way

when singing a phrase, we sometimes sing with the first note and not the later ones. instead, breath for the last note and that way it'll carry me through

there's something beautiful about the fact that to do the thing well you have to really _be_ with it moment by moment

whether or not you're intimately connected to the process has direct impact on outcome

one thing i like about making new friends is that i get to create myself anew

sometimes what keeps us stuck is a feeling of constraint around those whom we've already formed what i'll call a 'habit of knowing' around

encountering new people allows us, to a certain extent, 'play as' the person we're aspiring towards without past baggage

this new social space created between you and the person has different 'rules' and 'constraints' than those you've known for a long while

rules get stacked up overtime, creating patterns of interaction

not at all always bad, often actually really, really good!
(by rules and constraints i mean things that both generate and limit behaviour)

that new space is a liminal one (people dump on this word but i'll defend my boy turner til time's end) 

this means things are in flux and you can create new rules which help stabilize those aspirational qualities

ofc important to not create unsustainable rules

nothing's stopping you from more intentionally presenting yourself noticeably differently to those you already know! 

i think particularly attuned relationships in fact encourage that and can hold that change well

just sometimes hard to redirect old systems

in fact, i think contextual changes demand different behaviours - being logically consistent across all situations might not actually make sense as a way of being https://t.co/vwO69jacgi
from an ~ifs type view, seemingly paradoxical behaviours aren't actually paradoxical - through the frame of Parts, it _is_ actually "logical"

from another view, there's a theme of good teachers saying how they must adapt their instruction to be best fitted to the student 

in different situations, in some sense we too become different people, thus the "best" thing for us to do differs

relatedly, vervaeke talks about how virtues (e.g being honest or kind) can be viewed not as a list of things to maximize all at once

as he says, if we're being maximally honest all the time, it'll sometimes result in cruelty

so instead it's about getting an optimal _relationship_ between them - they are _interdependent_ 

a wise person is one who knows which virtue to best apply in which context

this is reminding me of when i formulated this very rough, very high level model related to making a class / course

is probably totally obvious to anyone who's actually thought deeply about education (e.g katrina lol), but i'd be curious for thoughts nonetheless https://t.co/hnLs0BdIOg https://t.co/ogQZWbvkxV

it came out of a conversation 2-3 months ago where i was thinking about how it must be possible to foster environments that more consistently cultivate creators who love what they do and their work shows it

if i were to lead my own classes, how would i do it?
i go to a decent art school (which is the very specific context that i'll be referring to), a place where you'd think that everyone who ends up here actually wants to be here and makes Good Work

but too many a times have i see mediocre work that is not only technically inept, but more importantly has no heart 

i've seen people make work that has not really changed in the 3 years that they've been at it for
i used to judge them but now i'm much more curious about what how to create containers that would lead to better outcomes (to put it in a sterile way)

didn't end up with concrete answers, but i think for me it goes back to that heart thing - you're (at the very least) interested in and curious about what you're doing, if not possessing a fire in your belly

from this flows motivation, and from that want flows everything else

for instance, on the technicality and heart point, atm i think that if you love it enough, technicality will naturally come because you'll seek it out if it's important to the work you're doing - foundations takes care of itself
(foundations -> heart works, but i think the other way around is much more potent)
as we were discussing, one of my friends brought up a class that they had taken where everyone *was* motivated to make cool stuff, but because of how the class was ran ...
... it ended up being just 'bleugh' and very disappointing - that was until the final class where the everyone actually got to hang out and work together, and suddenly there was a felt and alive bond
so that crystallised for me the notion that the second 'stage' is where students 1. create work that is genuine for them, and ...

2. are in an environment where they're surrounded by people who are doing the same + where everyone is inspiring and pushing one another to reach for their own highest potential in a mutually virtuous cycle

ok, to explain the model (here it is again)

i think in creating and leading a course in my school, there's (definitely at least) two broad parts i'd have to consider https://t.co/wnaNF1ahG5
'Part A' is how do i get students to actually want to be there, and then 'Part B' is how do i steward, on an individual level, deep work, and on a collective level, a community of loving peers

to what katrina was talking about, i think trust (mostly part of Part B) allows people to want to be there (Part A)

it creates the necessary base on which the 'deeper stuff' can happen

back to making courses, i'm currently thinking that the best i can do is to create a container within which stuff happens

rather than a technical, step by step everyone does the same thing kind of course (very valuable in many subjects! but not what i'm talking about)...
... it necessarily has to adapt and adjust in detail - overall structure and assignments can remain the same, but maybe way in which things are presented week by week, criterias for work etc shift

additionally, i think the tools / techniques / 'modules' that i can utilise for Part A can be 'coarser' to achieve the goal of "i want to be here!", as opposed to the necessarily 'subtle' and sensitive work to achieve Part B's goal

Part A's 'techniques' are maybe good but insufficient for Part B, but are 'coarser' so more readily plug-and-play and less dependent on teacher / facilitator

Part B's stuff is difficult and requires experience, but can be very powerfully applied 'backwards' to Part A domain
some examples might be that Part A has well-crafted assignments and thoughtful feedback questions / forms for the teacher, whereas ...
... Part B might involve how that assignment is presented and then later how the work done for it is critiqued, or how the room is arranged, or how the 1-on-1s with teachers take place

a big part of all this is that i don't expect Part B to be easily 'replicable' - it's really, really hard, and it's totally unreasonable to expect every teacher (even the great ones!) to be able to do that for every student in every class for every semester
but i feel like we ought to at least be able to get more people along the 'path' of Part A precisely because the 'techniques' of Part A are coarser and less teacher dependent for it to 'work'

now all of this is some silly thing thought up in my brain - i don't know if it's actually true that only coarse techniques can get most people through Part A - i don't even know what kinds of techniques are out there as a whole!

also, bc of the art school context, the kinds of things i'd teach are also baseline much easier to get my students excited about than e.g me in high school and been deeply uninterested in the sciences and in maths and figuring out how to get ppl like me to be into them

that been said part A feels achievable to a much higher extent than what's currently been done

also tbc, i really like my department and i think they're doing the best that they can and i can't fault them
but yeah, a lot of this is also motivated by looking at the geniuses that i've come across and being like "gosh how do we encourage the sprouting of more?"
natural talent is probably real but totally irrelevant to what i'm talking about - i'm pointing to heart and kick-ass collective energy

pedagogy is hard, inspiring people is hard, making genuine work is hard, facilitating a tight-knit class is hard

hard, hard, hard
but desperately important

sometimes i get shy watching a rainbow

that intimate embrace
reforming

ok eros 

i want to point at it because i think there's something to it about similar 'action' between eros + beauty

pulling from rollo may's love & will, eros can be thought of as a desire towards unity (in sex, in God, in knowledge etc), and it's defined by its 'desiring for'

(there's this bit where may talks about sisyphus being happy and i was like holy shit sisyphus is happy because he's perpetually driven by erotic love towards unity with boulder with the top 

i feel like eros is the most painful sort but who knows maybe sisyphus is a masochist)
esther perel talks about how sexual erotic desire is created precisely in the gap between you and your partner - there *has* to be distance, that tantalising not-having

i think desire might fundamentally operate on that push pull of there being potential of getting it, but it being unknown enough that it's absolutely not certain - there's always a threat of failure and loss

i think beauty maybe has to have that element of not being able to fully understand it - something about it resonates strongly yet goes beyond us, and so when we see it, it hits us with that je ne sais quoi and we're a bit (or a lot) in awe at how this could exist
if awe does have something to do with this then we can bring in ideas of awe pushing us towards and encountering mystery (vervaeke), which might be a very interesting line of thought

mystery is powerful in that it draws into the expansion of our being, and eros has in it a longing, a "forever reaching out, seeking to expand", pulling us towards it with a wanting for wholeness (love & will)

here are my reflections on parts of it: 
personality, making art, having containers for our (creative) insanity and more, in no particular order https://t.co/76wYWNWKOB

1.
we were talking about making art for art's sake and being less precious about our work, and there was this wonderful paraphrase of a freddie mercury quote:

"My songs are disposable like Bic razors"
on that, i feel compelled to say: i think that whether or not your art is """creative""" or """good""" is completely irrelevant to whether or not you ought to make it

i feel myself getting riled up defending all of you against your (outer and inner) critics who say that your art is shit and it's not "real Art" and that you should just never touch a pen / pencil / piano etc etc again

1 - fuck 'em
2 - i wrote a thread on what i think we mean when we say that a piece of art is "bad", and why you ought to make it anyway 

hopefully this might do something for some of you
https://t.co/vEDWaavIy7

to QT some parts of it
https://t.co/TUZkhQ5e01
https://t.co/dp26KpON0m
https://t.co/azVDoYHx1x

2.
SBK: "we're wired for creativity, but we're more deeply wired to deal with our safety", constantly trying to predict what's coming next

there's tension between feeling safe and to 'be creative' and do things that are in the unknown, emerging moment-by-moment (like in improv)

directly related to above:

SBK: key to creativity is emergence - you don't know where you're going until you get there

something very resonant about that and, as johnstone in impro might say, rather than trying to "be" original, we allow what's most alive and immediate and present to flow straight out produces the most 'original' and 'creative' stuff

3.
SBK brought up this idea that the Big 5 OCEAN personality traits has various aspects to it, e.g in extroversion it might either mean we get a lot of positive emotions out of socialising, and / or it might mean high assertiveness

i've been reading jung recently and this reminded me of him talking about how archetypes are never fully know and have many faces, so i had this thought of these 5 traits as living, multi-faceted attributes (deities, if you will) that influence us towards certain directions

4.
SBK - watered down mental illness actually correlates with creativity, so e.g relatives of those with e.g schizophrenia seem to be more creative than general population without debilitating aspects

this reminds me this really interesting essay on shamans, and there's maybe something in there about mental illness and giftedness and creativity and being a cultural force

https://t.co/nuucQZh1ES

5.
SBK talking about how generally, being high in openness makes us in part more open to just operating without a goal and to just pursue things for the sake of them, vs those who are high in conscientiousness who are honed into goals

this was very much a "holy shit that explains a lot about me 2-3 years ago" - goal orientated, could crank shit out, planning out my day hour by hour, love structure, wouldn't do things without a plan etc

6.
lots of discussion around neuroticism, one part was that neuroticism can actually help in our work - an example was given of overanalysing interpersonal relationships as being helpful for fiction writing, as well as having an outlet to transmute bad things that happen to us

and so maybe there's this idea of having containers for our insanity (or anything else!) to be expressed, and something about having e.g a 1 hour block each day to LARP as our most neurotic self and have it contained within that, separated from our 'normal' lives
the container would be important - with who? where? for how long? what sorts of things are you doing and / or not doing? how do you transition in and out? etc
from another angle, this could be 'used' to microdose a version of us that we want more of

here's this hilarious related tweet
https://t.co/Iw9rBUx0So

7.
SBK: you actually have to intrinsically want to change your personality, and so e.g when you're extroverted friends constantly force you (an introvert) to try go out more, that'll backfire!

(only comment is that i feel very vindicated :D)

i'm designing a social reading app! i'll be threading in some current ideas (all thoughts appreciated!)

i also have a short survey that i'd love for any of you who's interested in reading and in talking about ideas to take a look at - it'll greatly help guide me in what i make! https://t.co/AXpsnXTwrt

so, the idea for the app came from 2 things
1. lots of people want to get into reading / read more
2. lots of people (whether or not they read a lot) like talking about ideas

what if we used conversation as motivation for reading?

a potential powerful side-effect of this is that continually talking about those ideas helps us remember it better!

so, 1 thing i'm also thinking abt is how to encourage repeatedly referencing ideas and insights we've had - "natural" spaced repetition
i'll talk about that later, first i want to lay down 2 main guiding principles

1.

discussion is the central force that everything else in the app serves

good discussion sparks the connection of the mind and heart - i want to try push interfaces forward a little to better help with that

2.

books as first-class citizen

by books i really mean anything you'd want to talk about / take notes on, e.g articles, podcasts etc

to start with 1, i think there are 2 broad ways to help facilitate discussion:

1. top down ("events" like reading challenges, weekly open discussion question / prompt)
2. bottom up (book clubs, salons, book twitter threads)

(the distinction between the two is a little fuzzy)

each of these are 'entry points / avenues' into discussion - all valid ways of creating 'discussion spaces'

an aside on top down / bottom up is that they're 2 directions to think abt cultivating culture - one ideal is to have both work together, e.g 'leaders' having clear vision with that vision both influencing and being influenced by the collective's own creative output / emergence

i think @interintellect_ is a great example - @TheAnnaGat and team have been very deliberate as to the sort of community they want to build, and i'd assume they did some top-down things beyond just setting up a discord server in order to do that (curious to hear more about that!)
at the same time, there's a wide range of bottom-up salons that are consistently interesting, that "naturally" arise from the community that interintellect fostered
i also have more to say on this idea of creating community on app vs directly community, idea of 'events' and culture and a bit of christopher alexander, but i'll move on

so to be more explicit in the context of the app:

top down is 'admin' created things

e.g: the weekly open discussion question / prompt - each week, there's a new question like "what is love to you", and there's a space in the app to hold that discussion

bottom up is user created discussion spaces

e.g: the app makes it super easy to spin up book clubs and have interface specific to it, e.g comments are hidden based on what chapter you're on, or ways to capture ideas and references that the club might have during a call

and the book club can not just be private - you could imagine having the discussion in the club be public, but the members who can participate to be restricted - think of it like a text-based discussion panel for a book, extended over time
(you might see why top down / bottom up is fuzzy, because the team behind the app could invite well-known people to make one of these 'panels' that runs async for a week on a recent article)

another (more directly) bottom up approach is some analog to the book twitter threads

i want to be able to "live tweet" the book as i go and get input from those who've both read as well as not read the book

one idea is to be able to split the post into summary and insight - summary are ideas directly from the reading, insights are additional links, thoughts, refutations etc

this does a few things

one is that i think it might help encourage more active engagement with book, off of which you can start building up a 'library' of insights (you remember them better this way too!)

a second is you can more easily reference the ideas you've had

bit more on the second: the interesting thing about twitter is that the character constraint naturally lends itself to having 'atomic' ideas (cf matuschak on evergreen notes)

maybe the summary / insight split can create a different constraint that encourages the same thing?

a third surrounds idea of a 'book feed' - imagine that you've already read a book, loved it, and want to see other people's thoughts on it, but you don't want to see the same information again and again 

well, you'd be able to hide 'summary' notes and only see insights!

let's talk about 2 

my ideas surrounding this has one of its foundations heavily in andy matuschak's work and roam type wikilinks 

https://t.co/f20H5qnNZu

the basic idea behind books as first-class citizen is that ideally, the conversations on the app will be densely interlinked with ideas and insights people have gotten from what they've read / listened to
the interface should make it very easy to reference those ideas, and these links will be highlighted too so that there's a clear second layer of rich information that's beyond just the text itself

wait as i'm writing this, i'm realising i'm just reinventing academic paper references lmao 

but it'll hopefully be in a much friendlier and organic way, and with more context

anyways, what this will do is to encourage: 

- rabbit holes + 'natural' recommendation system on the end of the reader, and 

- revisiting past ideas, on the end of the writer (that 'natural' spaced repetition)

this is also the main impetus behind that summary / insight structure

one key thing though is that i don't want book notes to *have* to be the main place to create those insights - this app isn't a note-taking app - again, discussion is the central thing, not notes!

so one thing i'm trying to figure out is how to easily capture insights that you get from talking about stuff with other people (or from what other people have discussed) such that you can "quote tweet" it later, creating your 'library' of ideas, live, in discussion!
i'm thinking of a better interface to organise ideas that's a bit more than just having 'folders' so that it's more useful / has more context if wanted

another thing is thinking about the interface for discussion itself - this links into how we could reimagine chat, so, one prompt might be:

what is figma for discussion?

ok i have more to say on various details of the feed, of e.g people marking themselves as being open to talk about a book, of user profiles being sources of inspiration and so on, but i'll finish the thread here
very happy to talk to anyone about this, please feel free to message me! the timeline for me finishing designing A Thing is ~4 weeks, so i'll be heads down on this for a while

i spent 5 days in NYC living by "no thoughts just vibes" and i've been dragged kicking and screaming into the realisation that i'm not as introverted as i once was

i had to make peace with being an idiot and open myself up to the gratingly mundane things in life

some context: i scored 93% in introversion on that mbti site and thought it wasn't high enough

i've always liked my own company, was proud of being difficult to reach and hard to convince to mingle
otoh, the trope that with the right people the introvert won't shut up - that was true of me too
it's just i thought i had a hard limit of, like, 2 people, after which i'd be in the great danger of shrivelling up into a prune that had to be rehydrated in the solitude of an underground cavern

in NYC i got to meet my coworkers whom i've only known through zoom but that i already had great warm feelings for - they inspired this tweet 

https://t.co/aF9R3AjakL
when reflecting on my time spent with them, i realised that if they had invited me to go clubbing, *i'd actually do it* and that blew my tiny socially prune-d mind

friends - i don't know how to dance, i don't know how to ride a bunch of undirected (much less directed!!) drunk social energy, i don't know how to be thumped by extremely horny music at uncomfortable volumes for 3 hours straight and come out not feeling like a beached squid

i can't even get tipsy because i don't drink!

and yet, i was willing to say "fuck it" and get psychically blasted (while sober!!!) in the house of dionysus

how i understand the "why", 2 interconnected parts:

1. the stars aligned for safety such that i could lean into the chaos of both what i could be doing, and also loosen the grip on my self-perception enough to dip it into inky waters
2. like the missionary in a foreign country, i had an overarching purpose (directed at myself) under which all wills, moods, and activities served

on 1:

the exploring child only feels good about venturing into the unknown if there's the fallback of a loving embrace

freedom is only freeing if we feel safe to do so, otherwise it's freefall

https://t.co/TVybdAgmKE

there were 3 things that aligned, each of which gave a new degree of freedom:
- these are cool people that i like (outside-in)
- there aren't any expectations (co-created)
- my maximal vibes mission (inside-out)

my coworkers were 'new' people - there weren't any built up "habits of knowing" and so there were no expectation as to how i "normally" act

https://t.co/xN4FIikURU

these "habits of knowing", or 'cached perceptions', are *co-created* 

i actively create and reinforce an image while they, based on their image, put pressures on me to react in certain ways

relationships are built on top of these perceptions

change can be jarring to both sides because you'll need to once again find new ways to be each other - new "habits", new "rules"

what's more, *we ourselves* build who we are based on how others habitually "know" (encounter) us

so, to what extent are we willing to pull the bottom jenga piece out and let it all tumble down?

in that habits of knowing thread i talk about intentionally presenting a better version of myself so i can become it

among my coworkers, they instead *drew* something new *out* of me

and here's the thing - i don't actually know these people that well! i've only had a few zoom glimpses of them talking about their weekends, some lunches and a relaxing dinner - but in all of these we didn't talk about anything that deep!

i think one key was that it wasn't just one person, but an entire *group* of delightful people who had a palpable levity when in the presence of one another

they created a *social microclimate* that both coaxed out and consistently supported a certain way of being
and that whole thing of introverts maybe enjoying social interactions but getting tired - that one dinner i had with them, i didn't get tired!!!

there's this very sweet scene in 'night in the woods' where the cat saw the bear at a party and said that the bear didn't seem like a party person, to which he said that parties with gregg are different: https://t.co/xIoBDTnTPx
i've noticed that when i'm in certain social situations with people i know well, i can sometimes transfer my openness from them onto new people - i can 'act as if i were still safe in the corner'

however, because of the image i had created of myself among old friends as "the person who notoriously despises parties because the last time he went he was sat drinking water for 3 hours" (it was 5 whole years ago and it's still embarrassing, don't ask) ...

... if they were to ever see that person at a party, they'd realise they'd mistaken him for someone else because he'd never feel comfortable enough attending one with them there, even if he wanted to!

one way out is to be so charismatic that the cached perception is forced to update

well dear readers,,, i am not what you'd call charismatic lmao

with my coworkers however - there wasn't that expectation! 

and, importantly, i could trust that they'd be my corner and steward my awkwardness
and as part of all this, *i* had to be open to be a fool, and the space in me for that to happen was created by "no thoughts just vibes"

i read this fascinating essay some time ago, and part of it talks about the similarities between being a missionary and being a soldier

https://t.co/Uq59JRGKgv
in both roles, every action becomes meaningful - every decision and mistake is directly significant to the larger mission that both are serving
my overarching 'purpose' was a maxim that simplified and clarified how i ought to act in every situation, and in turn the kind of person that i should be to fulfill such a task

it opened me up to flow with "non-verbal signals of enjoyment", to more actively receive and return such signals

mrs c's thread is on small talk, but i think this mode is foundational for 'doing extroversion' and have it be fun and actually energising

https://t.co/svcnartH83
playfulness requires a fluidity of self that doesn't cling onto expectations

i can dance with my self-image because i don't need it to be some certain way, because i'm ok with being some guy that has a thousand forms, even if 997 of them are some variation of a bumbling idiot

https://t.co/BQE1OguOPo
keith johnstone said that we reject our first ideas because they're 1. psychotic 2. obscene 3. unoriginal, but actually the *best* ideas are thus
for me, i think the degree to which i'm willing to be completely insane is the degree to which i'm free
i'm being polemic of course, and respectability is fine, but i don't think i can respectability my way into not neurotically guarding how i'm seen by people
the sages are those who could be demons but choose not to be

ok, one last thing

i stayed with two dear, dear friends of mine who were wonderful hosts and took me to see many things over the weekend (like fireflies!!! i didn't know they existed in cities, much less NYC!!!)
i didn't have a private room to retreat to, and i think that actually had a big positive impact on keeping me from reverting back to my 'normal' self

there was a time when both of them were staying in the apartment i've been living in, and i wasn't as willingly sociable because my room was right there 

physical places exert psychic gravitational influences on my personality

every space is a container within which certain things are made more meaningful than others, certain actions better afforded than others

knowing how to deliberately nudge spaces so that particular versions of myself more easily show up is a good line of inquiry

and so i came home from the few days with a sense that i'm unfortunately not some rugged individualist, that i actually do enjoy the company of more than 2 people, and that, to my great terror, my wellbeing is disproportionality affected by nourishing social contact

i realised that i can't be a real individualist if i was never a collectivist, as i got there not through peace but through aversion

i'm still very happy to be alone, but there's magic that exists when with others that i can't yet get anywhere else

my studio lead gave a presentation about his story, and one of the things he said is that he's guided by "good food with good people"

that's really it, isn't it?

the question of meaning only really arises in situations akin to 3am, wide eyed existential ponderings where we're more busy figuring out what it means to be alive than actually *being* alive

https://t.co/Pb66uebiVJ

it's by no means an easy task - it took a great professor 50 hours to explain why it isn't, especially in our current age

maybe it never was, but in this disenchanted world, we no longer have spirits to comfort us nor gods to soothe our souls

we've always 'quested for transcendence', but i feel like the most transcendent thing i can do right now is to just "live laugh love"

such platitudes are seen as such because we only ever hear them with tired ears, and in our dismissal, they never get internalised
the most profound truths are banal, but in their depths they're like the name of God, unpronounceable

and so here's a wish - i hope to meet all of you and break bread one day, to dance in hell, to holler terribly into the mic about some lost love, and, in the waning moments of wake, to breath out one final thought:

isn't this what life's all about?

the main "experience" is short so you can more easily discern the whole situation
the "thing" is repeatable so there's also space to deepen initial relationships over time

(relationships of course evolve / deepen / complexify, so definitely not foolproof - e.g, might not vibe first time but really vibe 3rd time - hard to get to that point if low commitment

and i'm sure many other problems too)
also, this opens up readily inviting in non-members for short, one-off collaborations / cross-overs - can inject extra liveliness / perspectives / ideas without them having to commit a lot

other sorts of clubs seem to fit this (e.g casual sports clubs, game nights etc)

maybe podcasts are sort of like this? record a thing, see if it works, but can record more if good chemistry

curious for other examples!
