meditation log 009: 
awareness is unmediated - when i get stuck on thoughts, thoughts mediate / filter the rest of experience, which is why other sensory experience becomes more muted
i can notice the texture of awareness changing distinctly when it becomes mediated - it's often stuffy, heavy, but also in a way insulated, comfortable

today i very distinctly saw just how much movement there was in experience, and how attention keeps contracting onto those movements and getting stuck on them - often subtle when this happens, blink and I've spent who knows how long glommed to a line of thought

feeling really annoyed that my biggest emotional blocker around not being able to feel happy for others seems to be rooted in not being able to feel happy for myself for doing / being the same things, like what do you mean relational problems are actually personal problems???

there's a lot of tangles there and while it hasn't been much of an issue lately, it has been the dominant, recurring theme over the past few years and still shows up occasionally now, and it feels particularly frustrating because it actively blocks me from connection

and it didn't feel like i could just noodle on it on my own because those specific sets of feelings only come up in specific relationships, and when i've tried to troubleshoot it on e.g mdma it becomes so obviously not a problem (well of course i like them and am happy for them!)

with this new re-orientation though, there's maybe something like - i don't feel like i, or those whom i love, will delight in me being / doing the same things

and this isn't actually true because people *have* expressed joy in me, but it doesn't really sink in?

another problem with this is it's not just a single spot, it's the whole spectrum of expression, e.g certain kinds of earnestness, squishiness / weakness ...

and it's not just what's stated in the first post because it also requires a closeness for this to start rearing its head
and it doesn't always happen! i've had really close relationships where this isn't a problem at all - i suspect it happens when specific qualities of theirs mirrors some lack in mine (or something?)
to try voice it: "when i don't feel stable / secure in this quality of mine, other people's superior expression of this quality makes me feel [threatened / inferior ...?]"
and it's tangled because there's being secure in specific qualities and then there's general attachment flavored stuff / basic maslow needs of foundational security, foundational homed-ness
and this is tied into various flinch / cringe reactions, which in my case is pretty clearly one of "what i flinch away from in others is what i flinch away from in myself"

ah remembering something i heard, which is we flinch from someone either because they:

1. have something that i want but that i don't have
2. don't have something that i didn't want but that i'm forced to have

1) is the most obvious lens in my case, but i think 2) can also be applied too to the same problems, e.g instead of them having fluidity / ease with some aspect of themselves, framing it as they don't have the emotional stuckness / flinch that i have around the same thing
ah - looking back at a journal entry - if i simply brute force trying to love the person regardless, there's parts of me that will then say "why are you loving them when we're not getting that love?"

to hone in more on the actual sensations - it feels more like frustration, stuckness, tension, some active energetic buzz, red coloured thing (but not anger), wanting to get out of the situation

before i labelled the whole situation as jealousy / envy but that doesn't feel right

double backing though, i'm remembering that one area where this whole thing has unknotted a lot is around being smart / intellectual, and that *did* come from seeing that this was actually closely tied into a relational thing than just a self-worth thing

as in - much of my self worth was put into being smart and having a lot of intellectual concepts stored in my brain because that was the only way i knew how to get a lot of social approval / how i could get other people to value me

and in some areas that's definitely the case - e.g stuff around squishiness / weakness, having hard time loving that in other people because i don't feel like i can really express that myself due to perceived lack of external acceptance

a funny thing there is, there's this idea in some places that the things we think are scary / vulnerable to share  - people are not only actually very accepting of them and it in fact makes them feel closer to us

this seems to be broadly true, but for my own sore spots it actually *does* bring up rejection / flinch / judgement at the vulnerable thing of someone else

now i don't attribute it to be a problem of theirs, but still, it pulls me further away from them

so the scary thing is still actually scary because my internal experience validates that people (i.e me) *do* have rejection / flinch reactions to certain vulnerable sharings due to personal tangles

so that whole thing is kinda fucked lol
i can then return to the intuitive / obvious thing of only sharing such things with people i actually really trust and know their bodhisattva-ness than random person i just met at this event

anyways. hydra ass problem. the more i look at it, the more related-but-qualitatively-different aspects come up

it's going to be awesome once i've worked through this though, being able to really love people is so much more life-giving than stuck-frustrated-flight-red-blob

wait lol more puzzle pieces are coming up, specifically around all this stuff really only showing up in closer relationships - my default stance is very accepting, and if i get a full scope of the person up front it sorta stays that way

https://t.co/ebL17qVB5n

so the dynamic of threat only comes about in the unveiling of something that unbalances some internal model

people whom is emotionally felt as "superior" to begin with can't upset that balance, but a flip from "inferior" -> "superior" does

inferior/superior not as status, but more "is this person better than me at human-ing / has figured more of their shit out than me" - some part of it is, if the person is very similar to me i feel bad about myself for not being better, but this doesn't capture it all

ohhh wait a second - my tendency towards meekness and discomfort with being more domineering (powerful in relation to others, i.e leading groups) is an attempt to mitigate this whole dynamic

HMMMM

i'm beginning to wonder if the specifics of the qualities (weakness, earnestness) are symptoms and not root - they reflect shadows but there's some kind of more foundational dynamic that focusing on those particulars somewhat distract from

i feel like nowadays the most important thing a teacher of the arts can do is to bootstrap the student's taste

technical skill is important insofar as the student can actually have a vision for what they're trying to apply it towards, and it is interlinked e.g if you're interested in hip hop production learning technicalities of classical music won't be the most relevant first thing

to even know what teacher to go to so you can best learn what you want requires taste, e.g these two images are produced from schools of thought that have a very different way of understanding the technical fundamentals of drawing https://t.co/rwwwzdnuXP

one enables you to create highly precise visual replication that looks really good, the other to create highly structural images that better sets you up to draw from your head

(as an aside both interestingly have direct roots to 19th century french academic art)
the example above isn't the best because you can have a reason to choose one over the other based on the non-taste related reasons i gave, but hopefully the point is clear

you can't give somebody taste but you can only keep showing them things you think are good (and to speed it up / make it more solid explain exactly why you think that), and this is really important for most beginners because there isn't discernment yet

the finer the discernment is, the more it might aid the creation of the thing, not in the sense of analytical criticism of what you make but rather the telos that your work becomes organised and structured around into fulfilment

the question of if taste is necessary for great art spins off a totally different discussion - broadly i think it is (even if you can't articulate the why's etc), but there are cases where it's not needed (like most young children at certain kinds of art)

some ways to develop taste is to stare at a lot of good images / fashion fits / music, and really feel into why you like one thing and not another  - part of the reason why being around excellent peers help is the environment feeds you this constantly

another way is one of my favourite methods of being taught, which is analysis - what am I thinking about when I take this picture (this shape, this silhouette against this bright background), why does this composition work, and so on

i don't think the point is to set up some hyperverbal critic in your head that goes around Judging things, but rather 1) understand why certain elements / techniques do what they do and what impact they have on you and 2) see through the eyes of someone on a visual vibe level

if you know that the rule of thirds creates a nice balance, then you can intentionally disrupt it to make it feel off, rather than seeing or making an image and only vaguely feeling that something is off but not know why or how to change it towards something better
i've been also chewing on the tastes of "taste maker" / "curator" vs an artist, and there's some distinction there in what their tastes are serving and are for - can't quite articulate that yet tho

@WillBamberg yeah! so the basic gloss as i understand it is: the one on the left treats the artist as camera, you transfer shapes of light and dark onto the paper, use tools like plumb lines to get hyper precise measurements - their drawings can take 40 or even 100 hours, very meticulous

@WillBamberg the one i posted feels more solid but often works coming out of that school feels really soft to me - critics call the way they draw "tickling paper" because of how slowly & subtly they work
@WillBamberg the one on the right starts from a structural perspective - the face is made up of planes, and you approach it as such like so: https://t.co/ZRN2dIW608

@WillBamberg but really everything could be understood as planes, and these planes can be organised into something like a cube, cylinder, sphere etc, and those can in turn be organised into these bigger and more complex forms
@WillBamberg e.g here's an apple through a Chinese academic lens https://t.co/atXYtskSHM

@WillBamberg im heavily biased towards the one on the right because that's how i trained, and then i found out about the left school's way of thinking and found it interesting but ultimately very not helpful for drawing in a more generalist way

@WillBamberg this is why i don't like "drawing with the right side of the brain" as a book because it approaches the world solely through the left school's way, whereas the right school's way incorporates both from the start

@WillBamberg im a bit uncharitable here since the left school does study anatomy and sometimes thinks in planes etc, but my impression is it still centers on visual shapes than underlying structure of what you're drawing

@WillBamberg the downfall of the right school's way, more particularly modern outputs in china and russia where this specific flavour of drawing and cross hatch shading is dominant, is that it becomes too mechanical and it all looks the same

@WillBamberg while technically good (shows structure and organises light and shadow values in the work well), the images it produces are often not as beautiful as the left school's one - there's very little artistry in the places that trains this way
@WillBamberg i think this is less to do with the techniques than to do with the institutions, but don't have enough understanding to speak there

@WillBamberg this is a process image from Grand Central Atelier (left school) - their first step is to get the shapes and do a light / dark block out - the right school sorta does something like that too but emphasises the structure https://t.co/0zfPnkwK7p

since im turning into a full blown art account, please feel free to tweet at me / DM me about anything art related

wrt this - there's a few questions id ask first, like what art do you like? what art have you made before that feels resonant? what do you want out of it? https://t.co/8ReKk3ruAJ

what's your creative process, if you have one? who are your favourite artists?

some concrete suggestions might include going through a list of prompts and spending only 30 min each on them, or it might be to find a prompt you really like and approach it 100 ways for a month

it might be to buddy up with a friend and make stuff together, or find the first local art class that you vibe with and just do it

it might be to deliberately spend the first 50 hours only making bad art - be quick to reach the finish line and then dispose

examine your ideas of what creativity means and what you believe it takes to "be creative", examine your sense of if you want to be an artist or if that's not the point

read johnstone's Impro, read rubin's "The Creative Act", make a mess and do it all again

there's many ways ppl make stuff and a lot of reasons why one person sticks through one particular art medium and not another - all of this is deeply personal, which might include a relationship to art that's to simply be brilliant technical creator who's not interested in ideas

or it might be one who tries out 10 things and loves all of them, and for the rest of their life spends their time being pulled by and pulling together all of them into wonderful synthesis

and so on and so on
huh noticing i have a tendency to blab more and have to verbosely search around more with some people and not others ... what's up with that??

often in direct conflict with my desire to be precise and take up less unnecessary conversational space, which I think happens on its own if the other person is naturally more willing to talk for longer / in more detail

hm ok, I think a lot of my effort when i talk in those modes of elaborating more than i like is to have a lot of surface area / door handles for people to riff off on, so they can more easily get talking for longer about something they like

when it happens it's great because now you're really engaged and sharing, and when it doesn't and keeps on not happening well it just feels like im continuously saying too much stuff for no good reason

but what's the alternative?? just ... stare at them while i search for questions?

i do my best to ask all the questions that come to mind, but it's hard when i don't know which ones will get you going

ah! 

ask culture vs riff culture 

ask culture leans more on explicit question asking to reply ro, riff culture takes all input as stuff to actively jump off of

in speaking things that feel alive and not dead, i think I want to feel like our conversations are investigatingv stuff together

even when im saying something I've said before (some history of mine, some idea), I want to it feel fresh

this sometimes requires a continual rephrasing, reframing, rearticulation, as things i talk about can quickly feel stale, tired, like cardboard

hard to speak at all when that happens

just got on The Apps earlier this week out of curiosity and already noticing:

1) as advertised, filling out my profile helped solidify a snapshot of who i am atm

2) physical attraction rapidly became insufficient - had to have prompt answers that are interesting to me!

[...]

i could def see how these things can eat away at how you relate to potential romantic interests, but somehow it's pushed me to clarify what kind of person i feel drawn towards to even strike up a conversation, much less any potential romantic whatevers, which, really helpful!

""personality"" became even more strikingly important than before, and the way someone fills out their profile (if at all beyond pictures!!) feels like a strong signal of if they're *my* kind of people - if you don't have anything that i can reply guy well to, what's the point!

not sure how long ill keep poking at it - i do feel it tug on my attention in ways i don't like and also when i learnt about "standouts" (popular profiles pulled out that you have to use a special "rose" to swipe right on) i felt so repelled that i almost deleted it right then

makes total sense why they have the feature but it's so blatant what they're trying to do and what the app is made for that it all just leaves me feeling kinda gross

im also noticing the rhythm of the kinds of profiles they're showing to me - further reinforces above :(

all that aside, another neat thing is having bite sized opportunities to tune into what about this person actually spurs my curiosity, and having time to articulate that - this actually does feel good and useful!

joseph's one of the most clueless mfs in the bible so far, why would you tell your brothers these dreams when you KNOW they hate you lmao

alter notes that doublets are a "recurrent principle of organization in the Joseph story, just as binary divisions [are that of Jacob's]" - curious if the binary divisions have some further meaning / ties linking in with Jacob's character

wow the chapter where joseph reveals himself to his brothers made me tear up

what's continuing to strike me is the *humanness* of these stories - they're laden with symbols and archetypes, but they're also *real*, e.g jacob deals with the grief of his son & wife for decades

also to note is how joseph is tied closely to his display of emotion, weeping and flinging himself onto his family's necks

alter: "Joseph is at once the intellectual, dispassionate interpreter of dreams and central economic planner, and the man of powerful spontaneous feeling."

moses' great reluctance (refusal) to his call is neat because tasked with the grandiosity such task it takes all but the most courageous of people to meet it without struggle - moses, in this way, reflects the everyday man

the theme of moses being angry makes total sense - the continued breaking of instructions from God, despite seeing the ridiculous number of great miracles, is bafflingly frustrating at times tho understandable at others given that they were on the brink of starvation, thirst etc

done! quicker read since almost half of the book is technical material relating to the building of the tabernacle - i continue to enjoy the naturalistic back and forth details and negotiations, and also seeing the particulars of the story that are normally smoothed over

e.g: 
- aaron's verbal excuse of the gold calf simply appearing out of the fire after he threw gold into it vs the text describing his very deliberate fashioning of the idol

- moses receives the commandments, but the tablets he got on the second time around, which were then smashed, and then finally himself carves the new set of tablets on the third go

a lot of interesting details around this, and also two 40 days / 40 nights on a mountain

which actually reminds me of jesus, in his own 40 days / 40 nights in the desert, fascinatingly (and i'm sure by no mere coincidence) having the 1st temptation be bread (paralleling manna), and also spending time on the mountain top and dealing with idolatry

cool seeing moses going from the guy who escaped egypt and, with tremendous reluctance and continual doubt, step up as a leader who backs his people (one reading has God threatening to destroy Israel again and relents bc of moses), and being so holy that his face shone
now finally feel ready to dive into matthieu pageau's the language of creation book!

i'm mad that most music education seems to be utter bullshit and that i have to find and patchwork a curriculum together from the right esoteric jaded musician reddit comments to learn what i actually want to learn

take ear training for example - wade through a bunch of people telling you to "learn the intervals" before finally finding those who knows that that's bollocks and that you should learn it by scale degrees

next question: how??

i've been banging away at it with my own solution (hooktheory + piano), which is good but progress has been slow

and so after more digging i find out that this is actually what solfege is training you to do - to associate the sounds with a specific degree

which means i don't have to sing a note + separately feel into what it is since by doing solfege it directly binds the two together - "do" feels a particular way that's distinct from "mi", and "mi" will always be the third degree

construction and / or destruction ---> integration ---> spontaneity

if you only rely on spontaneity, you can get stuck on invisible self-imposed constraints 

e.g people do improv and implicitly try to set constraints on themselves of needing to be funny or interesting, which makes it worse!

you'd want both of these to get integrated / become second nature such that you now have an expanded source of spontaneity

doing "well" at improv is interesting because often in the best moments, "you" (conscious, controlling, planning self) didn't "do" anything

the "doing" is attuning into the scene, your partner, having fun, and letting the reactions flow

very common thing in improv is preplanning what you're going to say, and this often comes out of a desire for control and safety 

if i know what i'm going to say, then i can make sure that i'm [funny / accepted / not weird / etc]

few things to note, depending on what you're trying to do

if you're trying to be funny, preplanning stuff is almost the worst way to go - you get jammed into your head and disconnect from your partner, you're no longer RESPONSIVE to what is ALIVE

the funniest things come from finding TRUTH and following it, from being honest and pure about the character and scene you're inhabiting

focus on having fun, and being funny will naturally come as a side effect!
if you want to be accepted and not weird, an easy way out is to become bland and boring - you'll be "accepted" and then promptly forgotten about

mick napier in his book "improvise" talks about how you can follow all the "rules of improv" and still end up with very mediocre scenes - this is because:

removing all the bad things does not make something good 

it just makes it not-bad

what makes a scene good IS PRECISELY the naturally strange, quirky, unexpected things that come out of play, out of making BOLD choices (which can be committing to a staunchly silent character who doesn't move a lot and only gives one word replies!)

you can be fine by being bland and not-bad, but you become great by being TRUTHFUL to what is REAL, and reality (which YOU'RE a part of!) has an awful lot of fucking weird and wonderful details!

the best ideas are often the ones that are unoriginal, obscene, and psychotic!
i can either "manage my social anxiety" or simply become the kind of guy where having social anxiety doesn't make sense

i remember before signing up for improv that i was fretting about eventually needing to go on the stage and perform for the graduate shows (horrifying), but i realised that the whole point of why i was doing it was to grow to the point where that was just no longer an issue

and as i'm watching the shift slowly happen live, there's something like:

having fun is straightforwardly better than feeling awkward and anxious, so i will simply choose to have fun

it's sounds stupid because this feels very much like a "just become not depressed", but there's two things mushed together that makes this feasible:

seeing that there's a choice + knowing how to execute on that choice

both those things are very nontrivial because seeing the choice requires getting a taste that it is even possible, getting to see the other side of it

knowing how to execute on that choice is in the "skill issue" domain - it's building intuition around a bunch of things

it's not only knowing what to say when and how to practically navigate the situation, but also stuff like "even if the other person is being awkward i still know how to have fun", and that requires knowing what having fun MEANS TO YOU!
and part of that may very much require being in situations where, despite the other person being awkward, you're able to genuinely have a good time anyway!

good improv classes will throw you into these situations again and again, and also show you the vast possibilities of what happens when YOU having fun and following the fun and tuning into your partner ramps things up to heights that you didn't even know was possible!

or if you stick with it, more often than not it'll eventually lead to interesting places, or that more pieces of information are "entry points" than you realise, or even if it keeps being awkward, leaning into it and playing it up is its own kind of play

e.g there was this very bland scene with two people who had just climbed to the top of a mountain, and the teacher just let it keep on running, and it was like watching the worst first 5 minutes of a convo between two strangers crammed in 100 person, small apartment tech party

and then BAM one of them said "i think we need to get a divorce", and all of a sudden the dynamic completely shifts - who they are as characters clarifies, the scene gains life, intrigue, mystery, and the awkwardness makes sense in a way that it didn't before

a lot of scenes hinges on things like this - sometimes as dramatic as this, and sometimes much more subtle where the scene comes alive through a commitment to really picking up on what the other person is giving (gifting!!) you and riffing on it

clowning is fuckin' haaaaaaaaaard

improv is way less stressful in comparison - you don't have anywhere to hide in clowning, the flop and discomfort *is* the space where the clown exists, it's just you on the stage, alone, being a fool, AND you have to engage with the audience

it's a rigorous education in play - improv is too, but i think improv does it most directly in the social talky space whereas clowning does it in the physical - how you move, trip, slap people, and also how you transform a wooden stick into a dog's tail, a crush, a helicopter

(improv covers and impacts a lot more than just social talky stuff, but it's impact can be felt most obviously in the increase in play and social / emotional flexibility in conversation and riffing on things and hanging out with kids)

the clown is an animist and their world is alive - you treat objects like people and through that it reveals things to you

clowning is better thought of as a verb than a noun - you clown a conference, you clown your dad, you clown smoke

it's a theatre / performance art and so how you move is an entire thing - what does it mean to become a piece of paper falling, or to sit like the color red? how do you fake tripping over, how do you fake it with only your chest, or your hips?

we did this exercise twice where we had 20-30 minutes to fuck around with an object (a hairdryer, a colander, toilet paper), and then you go up on the stage for 2 minutes while the audience loudly "yays" what they find interesting, and "boos" the second they get bored

i found the "yay" / "boo" aspect pretty entertaining actually - fun as an audience and very cool to get immediate, authentic reaction based on what you're doing

what was hard was physical acting out idea after idea after idea and staying in the floundering and getting booed

in improv, majority of the time the audience disappears as you do your improv - you're deeply connected to the scene and your scene partners, but for clowns what they do is TO the audience and FOR the audience

and so the clown hides and reveals things to the audience, they react to them, they conduct the crowd 

there's of course ways of clowning where the fourth wall is not broken, but there feels something essential in clowning being about playing WITH the audience

and to be a really good clown you gotta REALLY know what you're clowning - only by knowing how people normally move can you then clown it into something stylized - same thing applies to the subject matters that clowns do

there's a real authenticity that has to be at play, a vulnerability of this very role of the fool, and the courage (in some of its best moments) to walk the tight rope to uncover and clown the truth of what's at the edge of our social / cultural / moral sensibilities

and i've just had the tiniest little taste, i can already see how deep the discipline goes - haven't even gone into mime or masks or buffoon yet!

i don't know if i'll do more anytime soon, feels like i want to do more improv before honing my character craft through clowning

i think it's also that i haven't yet investigated much at all and don't know what really good clowning looks like, so rn i don't feel particularly inspired to go deep in it, but i can feel it pushing hard on my edges and its direct relevance to everything i'm doing atm

ive done a 180 in my interest in Johnstone's work on status after watching a heart wrenching  improvised scene created just from leaning into the different status behaviours and modulating it

i still think it's a terrible lens to view normal interactions through and as some kind of practiced relational evo-psychy "skill"

but for theatre and drama, it's a brilliant tool that quickly and naturally creates particular relationships + stirs real emotion in the audience

as a technique, it's also useful to throw out the idea of status altogether (you'll get in your head and come with too much baggage) and instead just do the behaviours, e.g keeping your head (very) still while talking, or laughing + moving your head a lot + flickering eye contact

physical behaviours are not only for you as a character (e.g hunched back), but also for aiding how you react, which is the real core: 

a character forms from how they respond to others / situations / the world

it's not the only way to make great scenes though, e.g tightly matching status (such as between great friends etc), and indeed especially for beginner improvisers, being of the same status can help you improvise better as it removes some problems that come with the dynamics

@Jeanvaljean689 johnstone himself said that status isn't the best word for it but he never found anything better, part of the reason being it comes loaded with a bunch of things that he didn't really want attached

for a lot of status plays in theatre, there is that safety piece behind it

@Jeanvaljean689 but for some, the e.g forwardness and space taking can be something more like ... a "knowing" of how to deal with the person (a lack of threat, and so related to safety but without that piece of providing or removing it - the power is in self capacity / regulation)
@Jeanvaljean689 for lower status people, it can lean into feebleness, which can have people feel sorry for you because of your lack of capacity to do things, to be agentic, to stand up for yourself

@Jeanvaljean689 it's more clear in this low status example that there's alternative reasons that's not safety, and mainly all of this is to help expand the range of what status play can do in the scene, where e.g you can have high confidence, self assured father turn into weak and pitiful

@Jeanvaljean689 and the weak / pitiful comes from hearing the news that his family has decided to place him in a nursing home, and his demeanor slowly transforms over time on stage as the news sinks in, and the son whose delivering this news flips his own from low into high
@Jeanvaljean689 anyways, saying all this, i think it'd actually be really interesting to try play with having the safety piece in mind and having that build on top of the status building blocks!

1) recognition of how the world works 
2) development of inner capacity to swing with whatever situation i find myself in

the things in 1) that's been really helpful have been funny things like "rules are fake" and "everything is a potential gift (even awkward silences)" and "if you expand rather than contract when you make a mistake, people admire you for it"

which, that last part is kinda bonkers if you can rewire yourself to become energized by mistakes / flops (~~~EK, clowning) because then you're unstoppable - a lot of the reasons to not do something stems from fucking something up, but turns out most consequences are meaningless

there's rules that make a lot of sense and then a bunch of rules that maintain a polite society but much of the most brilliant, hilarious, beautiful, *human* things come from moments that trespass those boundaries into the chaotic *everything* that's outside of it

if there's no danger and no failure, nothing interesting happens! rules put a cap on natural expressiveness

@poodvt and then decision maker acts out the line, and then says "what's next" or "and then?", and storyteller A just keeps on going

now, anytime decision maker doesn't like the offer, they say, in a high pitched, light tone with a smile on their face "nope!", and turn to storyteller B

@poodvt that "nope" is supposed to be a little ridiculous, because it's really critical that the rejection is not of the whole person but just of that one offer not being the thing the decision maker wants right now - they don't want the storyteller to completely stop playing!
@poodvt storyteller B then picks back up where storyteller A left off, and then you just keep going like this until the story comes to a natural conclusion!

@poodvt there's a few dynamics that you can really feel when actually doing it that isn't conveyed well through the instructions, like how having 3 people (2 storytellers) *really* changes the dynamic, releases the pressure, makes the whole thing collaborative

@poodvt 'cause it might be really difficult to keep the whole thing going, and so what you can do is to offer something ridiculous or strange to get the decision maker to say "nope", and to get storyteller B to take the reigns

this makes that "nope" also a lot less personal

@poodvt maybe the offer was good and reasonable, but the decision maker just wants to go in a different direction and get a fresh lens on the tale

the storyteller can then take a rest and watch it unfold

@poodvt it's also an exercise that helps you begin to hone in intuitions about what kind of narrative works for some people and what doesn't - you can ask them later on why they said "nope" to certain things and not to others, and maybe they have a reason (but maybe not! and that's fine!

@poodvt and then after a narrative is done you cycle who the decision maker is

when i did this, all of the three stories were really interesting in different ways! and hearing from the other classmates there seemed to be similar revelations

@poodvt there's at least two variations on this (one with a much bigger group, and one with two people) that i think of 
that can also work, but they have somewhat different dynamics at play

@poodvt the bigger group version is bit more brutal but can be a lot more instructive, and the two person version is just different and i think you don't learn about the same things 

my (somewhat strong) preference is this 3 person version, especially to start

not that they're in conflict or inferior or whatever, but at the point in my life when i found out about meditation i was obsessive about it for like ... 2-3 years despite it failing horribly to make any direct impact spiritually
honestly i'm glad that it didn't work out like that, i would've been even more insufferable than i already was - having it be so miserably ineffective is very humbling

it also single handedly created my staunch "no one true way" stance, like, i don't know man sometimes some things work great for some people and doesn't for others, i don't think your [insert ideology / modality / tradition / whatever] is the Best Thing For Everyone Always

and saying all of this i still think something in the direction of life-affirming / tantric nondualism is the thing that i'm looking for, and i still think meditation is very cool and real and i listen to michael taft's podcast and an occassional burbea with great joy

but if the way i have to get there is through staring at a piece of gravel and going "fuck yeah" or slight suffocation or getting booed at while i try to turn a piece of string into an oar, then a javelin, then a pull up bar, then that's wonderful and a gift i'll take gladly

"preparation puts you in the zone of confidence, [but] the ideal is that you go out there and you hit very little of it"

(martin short, "off camera with sam jones")

if you're just doing the stuff you pre-prepared, the person you're talking to could be anybody, and then you're just putting on an act

what you want is a sense of "a real team work" going on

"Plans are worthless, planning is priceless"
https://t.co/2IHzbBukoT

i think that's why someone who only brain dumps their schtick feels hard to engage with because you're a swappable person - it's not a dynamic relationship

one way is to do enough brain dump to give / get context (play the head), and then you free jazz jam the rest of the way
great improv is effortless, all players are followers following each other - there is no leader, only brick by brick reacting to the reaction

you "plan" (study, prepare, train, learn etc) in order to have substance behind how you spontaneously react, but in that moment of co-creation it's order *and* chaos, execution *and* emergence, because everything exists on that fresh, dynamic edge

a theme that keeps on repeating in these interviews with highly successful performers is that none of them feel like they've made it, that they've did it - there's a constant questioning of themselves, of their projects, they're never sure if it's going to work

i suppose what's unique as compared to other fields is that they're in the world of the creative arts, which in some ways demands of you (and just naturally of you demanding of yourself) this continual change, growth, creation of something new

i find it comforting - we are ourselves are, in many ways, our most wonderful creation, ceaselessly evolving and re-made - once we've think we've made it and we're done, we're dead in the water because then there's nothing left - the world is Known and becomes static

one thing that this list doesn't capture is that some of these items can serve as the single rule that drives everything else

e.g "delight your partner" has wide ramifications and can be the sole thing you keep in mind when playing with your partner(s) https://t.co/id8W1ZiGau

for instance, in this conversation, do i take relatively more space or lean back, do i tend towards agreeing with them a lot or get into an intellectual argument - all of these are contextual based on the person you're talking to, some will delight in one mode and not another

this can be illuminating because it really does depend - i didn't understand that there are people who actually really enjoy someone being combative with them!

it gets you out of your head as your attention is on the other person to attune to what's exciting for them

"delight your partner" is particularly good because it directs you towards *what* to do with your attention - sure, you can be really sensitive to the person you're talking to, but then what? how do you figure out what kind of things to respond with? well, see how they react!

(important also, though, to pair this with the pointer on "having fun", so that you're attending to both the joy of others *and* the joy of yourself, which is much better than any single one alone because they cancel out unwanted side effects)

and in a scene, maybe the thing your scene partner wants *is* to be vilified or treated like shit or get into high status combat - this is ALSO a yes-and, given that they signal this!

the *players* can be relishing the experience while their *characters* are having an awful time

and for normal real world conversations, i find that i get a lot more from focusing on "how do i delight my partner" rather than trying to figure out how to appear smart or get them to like me or whatever, and funnily enough, if you do it with honesty, they'll naturally like you!

it's funny, people talk about sf and it keeps on being totally different from my impression of sf which is abundant hills and trees and beautiful sunsets and wonderful interesting people. i didn't talk to a single soul at a party here about ai until a whole year in!
and i mean even that conversation rapidly became about horses, which i'm still very pleased about

curate who you hang with and which parties you go to and that will shape your experience

sure, the likelihood of running into tech ppl is a lot higher than if you lived in wisconsin, but it's very possible to spend your whole time here not ever coming into real contact with them

when i first got here and was walking around with my parents in chinatown, a place that feels shockingly close to china, they commented that there are old folks who immigrated here via their children that spend their whole lives in that little block. they never have to leave!

there are entire subcultures! there are people who show up at the 6th avenue skate park almost every evening and that's a whole unique thing! i have no idea what they do for work or for fun outside of rollerskating and that's great!

like, i have to imagine most skateboarders here have much greater similarity with skateboarders in nyc or surfers in san diego than they do with the people who are in tech or in spiritual circles or live in group houses, and that's amazing, and that could be YOU too!

i often forget that a huge chunk of the population in cities like this are just regular people living their lives. there's a whole hispanic community that dominates the mission. mandarin speaking chinese people in both richmond and sunset districts. and much much more!

you can come to this city for the tech and the spiritual community (i personally came here in large part because of the alembic and the wacky diverse group of meditation-y people), and you won't be able to find it anywhere else, but it doesn't mean you're stuck with it!
the people from your past that you still think fondly of - childhood friends, teachers, caregivers, those youtubers you grew up with

my current two metrics for spiritual teachers are:

1) can they hang
2) do they fuck

tbc you can have 2 while being celibate, it's an energetic thing

i was struggling to come up with the right phrase for 1 - it's not that they're normal or even necessarily "grounded", but rather can they have a conversation with a normal person and not be Weird About It

a lot of spiritual teachers have this worbly high status aura around them that i don't trust, it's like if they were actually put in a humiliating situation they'll flip, and this tension ripples into day to day interactions

2 feels more self explanatory - are they in the world, embodied, connected to the soul and it's gritty parts, expressive and alive, do they actually *like* other people and not in some detached-from-above way

these are metrics that help me go down the paths that i care about, you probably have something different! 

it came up as i thought abt how the ppl i admire have changed, & also an experience of being in this movement & dance influenced improv class with older folks where [...]

being in the place that i am (bay area), i would've expected more ~hippy slant on the whole thing, and for the first few hours it was looking like the case, but then at one point they started swearing and insulting each other (grounded in real observation!) as demonstrations

and that made me trust whatever they were doing far far more! 

and they all ended up being tremendously playful, bombastic, vulgar, delicate, sensitive - they clearly at once didn't take themselves seriously and also were very committed to the truth of whatever they were doing

it shows up in a bunch of places - for instance, there's this idea that saying the vulnerable thing actually gets people to feel closer to you

but then you're replacing one ghost - "if i say vulnerable thing, they (the ghosts) will hate me" - with another: "they'll like me more"

text:
this is dangerous as there's actually a lot of cases where saying the vulnerable thing *does* make the other person judge you harder

there's also cases where it does indeed make them feel closer

either way it's unreliable, and the responsibility is placed outside of yourself

raw.full_text:
i feel like i needed to push a lot of this by myself a good amount so i have a good understanding of my inner landscape

it's like reading a book for the first time and doing analysis on the fly vs having already read it through, thought about it, & discussing it afterwards

text:

or let's say piano. you *could* improvise from the very start, but only doing that (the "end result" of idea -> execution) is going to be a lot harder than if you had technical fundamentals

text:
each thing you learn also has different thresholds - growth then plateau, growth then plato plateau - each part of this is a new 80/20 "idea -> execution"

e.g (1) basics of learning how to draw (hand-eye coordination), (2) learning how to draw from your head

each thing you learn also has different thresholds - growth then plateau, growth then plato plateau - each part of this is a new 80/20 "idea -> execution"

text: for a lot of these there's a shared foundation, and then a skill tree opens up with multiple paths to 80/20 (e.g illustrating characters vs landscape). but you can't really skip the foundation, and sometimes (like violin), the foundation takes a long, long time

having many different kinds of (1) all at once multiplies coolness (e.g chef AND makes instruments AND skilled at tango AND raps)

text: coolness is always social / relational. what's cool in one group of people will not be in another, e.g material markers of wealth (big cars and boats), or in our circles a lot of knowledge about buddhist scriptures (while not anti-cool it's more common, so loses uniqueness)

text:
we're improvising all the time but the reason why doing improv is scary is because most situations we're in are very familiar, and in fact when we're not many of us hunker down into familiarity as a way of control

good improv leans *into* chaos and plays with it

raw.full_text:
we're improvising all the time but the reason why doing improv is scary is because most situations we're in are very familiar, and in fact when we're not many of us hunker down into familiarity as a way of control

good improv leans *into* chaos and plays with it

text: improv is, then, a way to learn how to respond in a spontaneous, un-habituated way in situations both familiar (breaking personal patterns) and unfamiliar (taking in playing with what is fresh), expanding our range of expression + comfort with discomfort

raw.full_text: improv is, then, a way to learn how to respond in a spontaneous, un-habituated way in situations both familiar (breaking personal patterns) and unfamiliar (taking in playing with what is fresh), expanding our range of expression + comfort with discomfort

text:
btw - familiarity for someone could be "always doing the weird strange thing", and for that person staying in one place and being in a routine is what's uncomfortable and unfamiliar for them

what if i played someone who was boring? what if i don't nod to anything they say?

internal and external signals are all signals that show up in the body

text:
I made my first video essay!! 

Good art provokes you into experiencing the beauty of your own perception

It's about:
- art and attention + presence
- art history as a progression of how our attention has changed (and some details on that history)
- viewing art as a way to inhabit the artist's world and see through their sight to expand and deepen ourselves
- games
- and more!

Timestamps:
00:00 - good art provokes us into the here-now, in an embodied way, to consider the things that matter
01:06 - art history is a study in how our attention has changed over time
01:39 - art as inhabiting the world of the artist from their perspective
02:26 - flattening of the value hierarchy: from Jesus the King to just the color red
03:05 - Medieval art: spiritual hierarchy
03:33 - the Renaissance: the dignity of the human
04:20 - the Baroque: the Catholic Church engineers attention
05:06 - the Dutch Golden Age: where modernity, domesticity, and the stock market were born
06:24 - Romanticism: meaning after God
06:42 - Realism: the unadorned truth of ordinary life
07:05 - Impressionism: painting how perception feels
07:53 - the Cambrian explosion of modern art
08:21 - Rothko paints landscapes of meaning
08:47 - Cubism: embodied seeing
10:33 - Hockney's collages: seeing as fragmented snapshots
10:44 - Light and Space: the environment is never neutral
12:01 - John Cage's 4'33": the coughing is the music
12:53 - perception itself is the art act
13:32 - Marina Abramović: presence as medium
14:10 - games as crafted modes of being, transforming through play
15:28 - metaphor versus presence
16:05 - being here now is uncomfortable, and that’s what makes you alive
17:00 - a little more aware of how beautiful the world is

text:
002: The beliefs that run your life are the ones you cannot see

New video essay on:
- how to see the invisible beliefs that influence you
- rituals, self as little god
- dehabituating perception, seeing holy cows
- steering with curiosity & boredom to find your deepest values
- play through disciplined experimentation to systematically change your life
- & more!

This one’s quite dense, so I’ve made an outline that’ll hopefully help you track all the moving pieces - link to it is in the next tweet, which also has everything else like the transcript and references.

01:54 - the belief diagram

06:11 - the diagram are diagnostic lenses
06:26 - [Chapter 2] Seeing the invisible beliefs that run us
06:33 - the sacred & the profane

13:28 - resources for untangling emotional knots

text: @majamediaco for example "double clicking" on something is a very understandable metaphor now, because we're internet native culture. I think the younger gen z and gen alpha can grok media in ways that I just can't comprehend, because they grew up in it

text: @majamediaco but culture is very slow to move - we still have a "desktop" with "folders" and a "trash can", we have "windows" with "tabs" - all physical metaphors for a digital space. necessary in its inception, but limits what we can do with it

raw.full_text: @majamediaco but culture is very slow to move - we still have a "desktop" with "folders" and a "trash can", we have "windows" with "tabs" - all physical metaphors for a digital space. necessary in its inception, but limits what we can do with it
